message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 9
Recent Green phase: none - test 9 (4 runes → 115 G) passed immediately because existing guard requires exactly 3 runes

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the architecture notes, test file, and implementation file to understand the current state.

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: extracted module-level constant `PROCESSING_FEE = 5` in `src/claim-office.ts`; `quote()` now returns `PROCESSING_FEE`
5	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the literal `5` is the domain "processing fee" from the spec; naming it removes a magic number
6	- Naming established: `PROCESSING_FEE` (module-private constant); `quote(input)` public function kept as-is — name still fits
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- No refactoring (justified): only 2 data points (empty=5, single potion newcomer=49); the literal `49` is hardcoded and any decomposition into `base + first-insurance + fee` components would be speculative triangulation beyond what tests demand. Naming `49` (e.g. `POTION_NEWCOMER_PREMIUM`) would not add intent beyond the test name and would clutter the module before the algorithm emerges in cycle 3+ (amulet test). `quote` name still fits.
10	
11	## After cycle 3
12	- Change: replaced `if (type === "amulet") return 71; return 49;` chain with a `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE: Record<string, number>` lookup table (`potion: 49, amulet: 71`); `quote` now does a single map lookup on `items[0].type`
13	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — premium varies per item type, so a per-type lookup is the natural shape and removes a conditional chain that would have grown with staff/sword. Also nets Rule 4 (one fewer conditional, mass 23→22). Kept totals (49, 71) un-decomposed: tests do not yet pin down the `base + base/10 + fee` formula; decomposition deferred to cycle 4 (staff) or 5 (sword) when a 3rd/4th data point makes the pattern undeniable
14	- Naming established: `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` (module-private lookup); `quote` name still fits
15	
16	## After cycle 4
17	- Change: decomposed opaque per-type totals into the domain formula. `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` (49/71/93) → `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (40/60/80) plus new constant `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.1`. `quote` now computes `basePrice + basePrice * 0.1 + PROCESSING_FEE` for non-empty item lists.
18	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — with the 3rd data point (staff 80→93) in place, all three test names explicitly state the same `base + first-insurance + fee` decomposition; the implementation now mirrors the spec language instead of hiding the formula behind precomputed totals. Also Rule 3 (DRY): the surcharge+fee pattern was conceptually duplicated across all three map entries; now expressed once. Mass increased ~25→~33 — accepted because Rule 2 > APP. Verified all 3 base prices (40, 60, 80) and the upcoming sword case (100) yield integer results under `base*0.1`, so no rounding decision was committed (rounding rule for rune 25→33 deferred to its own cycle).
19	- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (replaces `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE`), `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` (new domain constant); `quote` name still fits
20	
21	## After cycle 5
22	- No refactoring (justified): sword test added one row (`sword: 100`) to `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` — the cycle-4 decomposition absorbed it without any structural change. Considered inlining `firstInsuranceSurcharge` into `basePrice * (1 + RATE)` for Rule 4 (fewest elements), rejected because test names enumerate three distinct components ("100 base + 10 first-insurance + 5 fee") and Rule 2 (reveals intent) > APP. Considered extracting a `priceForFirstItem(type)` helper, rejected as speculative — no second-item or modifier test active yet. `quote` name still fits.
23	
24	## After cycle 6
25	- Change: extracted `const premium = basePrice + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE` so the final return reads `return Math.ceil(premium)` instead of `Math.ceil(basePrice + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE)`. Also added `rune: 25` to `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (in Green); the structural fit was already there.
26	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the rune test introduced rounding-up as a domain rule (25 × 1.1 + 5 = 32.5 → 33). Naming `premium` makes the rounding step a visible, named operation ("round the premium up to a whole gold piece") rather than a wrapper around a raw arithmetic expression. Mass +1 binding (~35→36), accepted on the cycle-4 precedent that Rule 2 > APP when test vocabulary ("premium ... rounded up") explicitly names the value. Considered leaving the inline form for Rule 4 (fewest elements) — rejected because the rounding rule's target deserves a name now that rounding exists. Considered renaming `quote` itself (e.g. to `computePremium`) — rejected, "quote" is the domain action and the function still returns the quoted premium amount.
27	- Naming established: `premium` (local binding for the pre-rounded total); `quote` and existing constants unchanged.
28	
29	## After cycle 7
30	- Change: removed the empty-items early-return guard (`if (input.items.length === 0) return PROCESSING_FEE;`). With cycle-7 Green generalising `basePrice` to `items.reduce(..., 0)`, the empty case now falls out of the same formula: `0 + 0*0.1 + 5 = 5` (and `Math.ceil(5) = 5`). One uniform code path handles all item counts.
31	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 4 (fewest elements) — the special case became dead code the moment `basePrice` became an accumulator over `items`. Also nets Rule 2 (reveals intent): a single formula expresses the domain rule "premium = ceil(sum-of-base-prices × 1.1 + processing fee)" for every item-count, rather than splitting "empty" and "non-empty" branches. Mass drop ~−9 (one conditional, one length access, one equality, one literal `0`). Considered keeping the guard for "documentation value" — rejected: the empty test name ("empty item list → premium 5 G (processing fee only)") is now an emergent consequence of the general formula, which is stronger evidence of correctness than a hand-coded shortcut. Naming all still fits.
32	- Naming established: none new; removed elements only.
33	
34	## After cycle 8
35	- Change: extracted the literal `60` in the 3-runes guard into module-level constant `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE = 60`. Body now reads `isThreeAlikeRunes ? THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE : rawBasePrice` instead of `... ? 60 : rawBasePrice`. Implementation shape (narrow guard `isThreeAlikeRunes`) left intact.
36	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the literal `60` is the domain "building block price for 3 runes" per Example Mapping; naming removes a magic number and the visual collision with `amulet: 60` (different concepts, same value). Mass +1 (one new constant binding at module scope), accepted on Rule 2 > APP precedent. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes` → `hasRuneBuildingBlock` and generalising to a per-block discount (would scale to cycles 9-12: 4 runes, 7 runes, mixed rune/moonstone). Rejected as speculative — next test ("4 runes → no block") will force the generalisation with concrete data; doing it now would be triangulation. The narrow guard's name remains accurate for the single data point we have. Considered also extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` into a named predicate function — rejected, it's used once and inlining preserves locality.
37	- Naming established: `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE` (module-private constant); `isThreeAlikeRunes`, `quote`, and other existing names unchanged.
38

content.text:
All 9 tests pass. Now let me evaluate refactoring opportunities for cycle 9.

## Refactor Phase Analysis — Cycle 9 (4 runes → 115 G)

### Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Current names in scope:
- `quote` — function name. Domain action ("quote the premium"), still fits.
- `rawBasePrice` — pre-block-discount sum across items. Still fits but contextually narrow.
- `isThreeAlikeRunes` — boolean guard for exactly 3 runes. **This name is now under scrutiny:** with the 4-runes test (which is the *negative* case — "4 runes (no block — requires exactly 3) → 100 G base"), the predicate's meaning is now confirmed as specifically *exactly* 3 runes. The name `isThreeAlikeRunes` already captures "three" exactly, so it remains accurate.
- `basePrice`, `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `premium`, `PROCESSING_FEE`, `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE`, `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE`, `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE` — all remain accurate.

Naming Decision: **Keep all names**. The 4-runes test is the negative-case confirmation of the existing 3-runes guard; it did not change the meaning of any existing name (the guard already required exactly 3, and the test confirms that constraint).

### Step 2: Calculate Current APP Mass

```typescript
export function quote(input: { customer: { yearsWithMHPCO: number }; items: { type: string }[] }): number {
  const rawBasePrice = input.items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE[item.type], 0);
  const isThreeAlikeRunes = input.items.length === 3 && input.items.every((item) => item.type === "rune");
  const basePrice = isThreeAlikeRunes ? THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE : rawBasePrice;
  const firstInsuranceSurcharge = basePrice * FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE;
  const premium = basePrice + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE;
  return Math.ceil(premium);
}
```

Detailed component count (function body only):

Line 14 (`rawBasePrice = reduce`):
- Bindings: `rawBasePrice`, `sum`, `item` = 3
- Constants: `0` (initial), `"type"` access = 1
- Invocations: `.reduce`, `+` (inside callback), `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE[...]` (index access) = 3
- Property accesses (`input.items`, `item.type`) — treat as invocations (2 more) → 3+2 = 5 invocations × 2 = 10

Line 15 (`isThreeAlikeRunes`):
- Bindings: `isThreeAlikeRunes`, `item` = 2
- Constants: `3`, `"rune"` = 2
- Invocations: `.length`, `===`, `&&`, `.every`, `===` (inside callback), `item.type` access = 6 (× 2 = 12)
- Conditionals: `&&` short-circuit could count as 1 conditional = 4

Line 16 (ternary):
- Bindings: `basePrice` = 1
- Conditionals: `? :` = 1 × 4 = 4

Line 17:
- Bindings: `firstInsuranceSurcharge` = 1
- Invocations: `*` = 1 × 2 = 2

Line 18:
- Bindings: `premium` = 1
- Invocations: `+` × 2 = 2 × 2 = 4

Line 19:
- Invocations: `Math.ceil` = 1 × 2 = 2

This is getting hand-wavy. Using a more disciplined coarse count (consistent with prior cycles' "~36" estimate after cycle 6, then drop ~9 after cycle 7 to ~27, then +1 after cycle 8 to ~28):

**Estimated current mass: ~38** (the `isThreeAlikeRunes` line is heavy: a `===`, `&&`, `.every` with inner callback, and another `===`).

### Step 3: Evaluate Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** All 9 tests passing.

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** The body reads cleanly: compute raw base, check for 3-rune block, swap base if applicable, add surcharge, add fee, round up. The `isThreeAlikeRunes` line is the densest part.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication):** No duplication. The 4-runes test absorbed without code change — that's evidence the existing decomposition is correct.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** Possible candidates:
- Extract `isThreeAlikeRunes` predicate to a named function? Used once; inlining preserves locality. Reject (cycle-8 precedent).
- Inline `firstInsuranceSurcharge` into `basePrice * (1 + RATE)`? Rejected in cycle 5 because spec language enumerates three components. Still applies.
- Inline `rawBasePrice` into the ternary? Would create `(isThreeAlikeRunes ? THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE : items.reduce(...))` — collapses two bindings into one expression but the reduce is verbose enough that giving it a name aids readability. Reject.

### Looking deeper at the 4-runes test signal

The 4-runes test added **no implementation change** because the existing `length === 3` guard already excluded it. But the test name says "4 runes (no block — requires exactly 3)" — this confirms the *narrow* exactly-3 semantic of the current guard. There is also an upcoming test ("7 runes → 1 block of 3 + 4 individuals") that will force a *generalisation* from "exactly 3" to "as many blocks of 3 as fit, plus remainder." The current `isThreeAlikeRunes` guard is a placeholder that will be replaced (not extended) in cycle 10.

**Refactoring decision:** Doing speculative generalisation now (e.g. extracting a `runeBlockPricing(items)` helper, or computing `floor(runeCount/3)` blocks) would be triangulation beyond what the current 9 tests demand. The cycle-8 architecture note already considered and rejected this generalisation explicitly: *"next test ('4 runes → no block') will force the generalisation with concrete data; doing it now would be triangulation."* But the 4-runes test turned out to be a *negative* confirmation of the existing narrow guard, not a forcing test. The 7-runes test will be the actual forcing test.

### Considering one targeted improvement

The `input.items` is referenced three times in the body (line 14, line 15 twice). Could extract `const items = input.items` to:
- DRY (Rule 3): three repeated `input.items` accesses become one
- Slight mass reduction (~−4: drops two `input.items` property accesses)
- Marginally clearer (line 15 becomes `items.length === 3 && items.every(...)`)

This is small but real, and is justified locally (not speculative — the duplication exists *now* in the current 9-test code, not in some future code).

Let me apply it.

input.new_string:
## After cycle 8
- Change: extracted the literal `60` in the 3-runes guard into module-level constant `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE = 60`. Body now reads `isThreeAlikeRunes ? THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE : rawBasePrice` instead of `... ? 60 : rawBasePrice`. Implementation shape (narrow guard `isThreeAlikeRunes`) left intact.
- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the literal `60` is the domain "building block price for 3 runes" per Example Mapping; naming removes a magic number and the visual collision with `amulet: 60` (different concepts, same value). Mass +1 (one new constant binding at module scope), accepted on Rule 2 > APP precedent. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes` → `hasRuneBuildingBlock` and generalising to a per-block discount (would scale to cycles 9-12: 4 runes, 7 runes, mixed rune/moonstone). Rejected as speculative — next test ("4 runes → no block") will force the generalisation with concrete data; doing it now would be triangulation. The narrow guard's name remains accurate for the single data point we have. Considered also extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` into a named predicate function — rejected, it's used once and inlining preserves locality.
- Naming established: `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE` (module-private constant); `isThreeAlikeRunes`, `quote`, and other existing names unchanged.

## After cycle 9
- Change: extracted `const items = input.items` at the top of `quote` to remove three repeated `input.items` accesses across the reduce, the length check, and the every-call. Body now reads `items.reduce(...)`, `items.length === 3 && items.every(...)`.
- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 3 (no duplication) — three identical property accesses become one named binding; the predicate line in particular reads more cleanly without the repeated `input.` prefix. Small APP win (~−3 mass: drop two `input.items` accesses, add one binding). The 4-runes test added no implementation change — the cycle-8 narrow guard `length === 3` already excluded it — confirming that test 9 is the negative-case confirmation, NOT the forcing test for generalisation. Considered (and rejected) generalising the rune-block logic to `floor(runeCount/3)` blocks now; that is speculative triangulation. The 7-runes cycle (cycle 10, currently `it.todo` "7 runes (1 block of 3 + 4 individuals) → 175 G base") is the genuine forcing test for that generalisation. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes`: name still accurate after a negative-case confirmation. Considered extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` to a named function or inlining `rawBasePrice`: both rejected on cycle-8 precedent (locality / verbose-expression-deserves-a-name respectively).
- Naming established: `items` (local binding aliasing `input.items`); no other name changes.

### Step 7: Report Completion

Refactor Phase Complete (cycle 9 — 4 runes → 115 G):

- **Refactoring**: Extracted `const items = input.items` at the top of `quote` to consolidate three repeated `input.items` accesses into one named binding (Rule 3 — no duplication).
- **Mass Change**: ~38 → ~35 (net −3: removed two `.items` property accesses, added one binding).
- **Tests**: 9 passing, 29 todo. All green.
- **Naming**: All existing names (`quote`, `isThreeAlikeRunes`, `rawBasePrice`, `basePrice`, `premium`, `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE`, `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE`, `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE`, `PROCESSING_FEE`) still fit; only addition is `items` local binding.
- **Speculative generalisations deferred**: The rune-block `floor(n/3)` generalisation was not done — cycle 10 (7 runes → 175 G base) is its actual forcing test.
